subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child explores coins and starts to recognize pennies and dimes.
Your child can name pennies, nickels, and dimes and know how much each is worth.
Your child can recognize pennies, nickels, and dimes and knows their names and values.
Your child can recognize pennies, nickels, and dimes and knows how much each coin is worth.
Your child can name pennies, nickels, dimes, and quarters and know how much each coin is worth.
Your child can recognize pennies, nickels, dimes, and quarters.
Your child can recognize and name coins like pennies, nickels, dimes, and quarters.
Your child can name pennies, nickels, dimes, and quarters.
canonical_name: Kindergarten Math: Money — Recognizing Coins and Their Values
coherence_reason: All standards focus on recognizing, naming, and knowing the value of basic coins (pennies, nickels, dimes, quarters).

subject: Math
sample_standards: Model with mathematics.
Attend to precision.
Collaboration
Numbers and Operations
Engage in building activities
Identify numbers 1-5
Engage with numbers.
Engage with number models.
canonical_name: Kindergarten Math: Numbers and Operations — Number Recognition and Modeling
coherence_reason: The standards mix math practices, collaboration skills, and specific number recognition, indicating at least two distinct sub-concepts.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child understands how the War of 1812 and the Creek War affected Alabama socially, politically, and economically.
Your child knows who the leaders are in your town and state and what they do.
Your child understands why Europeans explored and settled in Alabama and how this affected trade, health, and land.
Your child can make daily schedules, calendars, and timelines to organize events.
Your child knows how the environment, land features, and weather affect how people live, like what they eat, play, and where they live.
Your child can name parts of clocks and tell and write time to the hour using both digital and analog clocks.
Your child can talk about patterns related to time using words about real events.
Your child understands how living things have both similarities and differences because of how they have changed over time.
canonical_name: Kindergarten Math: Time — Telling Time and Relating Events
coherence_reason: Several standards are about history and social studies, with only a few focusing on math concepts related to time and events.
split_topics: Historical events, leaders, environment, and change over time (Social Studies/Science)

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child is still learning and has not yet met the basic level for counting objects.
Your child is still learning and has not yet met the basic level for solving simple addition and subtraction problems.
Your child is still learning and has not yet met the basic level for comparing numbers.
Your child is still learning and has not yet met the basic level for breaking apart numbers.
Your child is still learning and has not yet met the basic level for adding and subtracting within 5.
Your child is still learning and has not yet met the basic level for finding numbers that add to 10.
Your child is still working toward meeting the basic counting skills expected at this level.
Your child is still learning and has not yet met the basic level for comparing groups of objects.
canonical_name: Kindergarten Math: Numbers and Operations — Counting, Comparing, and Basic Addition/Subtraction
coherence_reason: The standards cover related but distinct sub-concepts: counting, comparing, and basic addition/subtraction, which could be split for greater clarity.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child understands that addition means putting things together and subtraction means taking things apart or away.
Your child understands addition means putting together and subtraction means taking apart or away.
Your child understands that the minus sign (-) means taking things away.
Your child can show or explain that – means taking some away.
Your child can connect the minus (-) sign to words like take away, take apart, minus, and subtract.
Your child can explain or show that plus (+) means putting things together using objects and senses.
Your child will recognize and use the plus (+) and minus (-) signs.
Your child can recognize a number sentence that shows subtraction, like 3 - 1 = __.
canonical_name: Kindergarten Math: Addition and Subtraction — Meaning of Operations and Symbols
coherence_reason: All standards focus on understanding the meaning of addition and subtraction, including their associated symbols (+, -) and the concepts of putting together and taking apart.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can think about math problems both in general ideas and with real numbers.
Your child can explain their thinking clearly and listen to others' ideas to decide if they make sense.
Your child can understand problems and keep trying until they find a solution.
Your child can choose and use the right tools to help solve math problems.
Your child understands what rights people have and what they should do as citizens in your community and state.
Your child understands how to think carefully and solve problems.
Your child understands and uses the intermediate value and extreme value theorems to solve problems.
Your child can think about math problems in different ways, using numbers and real-life examples.
canonical_name: Kindergarten Math: Mathematical Practices — Problem Solving and Reasoning
coherence_reason: This cluster contains standards about mathematical problem solving and reasoning, but also includes a social studies standard about citizenship and an advanced high school math theorem, so the grouping is incoherent.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Career Research
Your child can copy, continue, and make simple patterns using real objects.
Understand simple patterns.
Your child will copy actions that show putting together and taking apart using objects and different senses.
Your child can focus on and play with one object.
Your child can make, continue, and explain repeating patterns with numbers and shapes.
Your child can recognize and create repeating patterns with numbers and shapes.
Your child can explain, continue, and create repeating patterns with up to four parts and describe time-related patterns.
canonical_name: Kindergarten Math: Patterns — Recognizing and Creating Repeating Patterns
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on recognizing, creating, and explaining repeating patterns, but a few (e.g., 'Career Research', focusing on one object, and actions of putting together/taking apart) are only tangentially related or off-topic.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can find and use the way things are organized or structured to help solve problems.
Your child can notice and explain patterns when solving problems repeatedly.
Your child can notice and explain patterns or repeated steps when solving problems.
Your child can notice and use the way math problems are put together to help solve them.
Your child can notice and talk about patterns they see when solving problems again and again.
Your child can explain why a pattern happens.
Your child will look for and use patterns or shapes to help understand math.
Your child learns about patterns, how things work, and simple algebra ideas.
canonical_name: Kindergarten Math: Mathematical Reasoning — Recognizing and Using Patterns
coherence_reason: All standards focus on recognizing, explaining, and using patterns or structure in mathematical problem solving.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can quickly add and subtract numbers up to 5.
Your child can practice adding and subtracting two numbers up to 5 using objects.
Your child can use a method to add or subtract two numbers up to 5 to find the answer.
Your child can quickly add and subtract numbers up to 5 using different ways to solve real problems.
Your child can quickly and accurately add and subtract numbers up to 5.
Your child can quickly add and subtract numbers within 5.
Your child can quickly add and subtract numbers up to 5 in their head by imagining groups and breaking numbers apart.
Your child can add and subtract numbers that are 5 or less.
canonical_name: Kindergarten Math: Addition and Subtraction — Fluency within 5
coherence_reason: All standards focus on fluently adding and subtracting numbers up to 5, using different methods or representations, but always within the same numerical range and operation types.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can build shapes using objects like sticks and clay and draw shapes they see around them.
Your child can build shapes using materials like sticks and clay and can draw shapes.
Your child can build or draw shapes to represent things they see around them.
Your child can enjoy playing with building materials.
Your child can follow step-by-step directions to use building materials to make a shape.
Your child enjoys exploring objects found around them.
Your child can name different building materials like sticks, clay, or pencils.
Your child can pick an object from their surroundings to talk about.
canonical_name: Kindergarten Math: Geometry — Building and Drawing Shapes
coherence_reason: Most standards address building and drawing shapes, but some focus on exploring materials or naming materials and objects, which are tangential to the main geometric concept.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can put simple shapes together to make bigger shapes.
Your child can put together flat or solid shapes to make bigger shapes and then create new shapes from those.
Your child can find the area of triangles, special four-sided shapes, and other polygons by breaking them into simpler shapes.
Your child can put flat shapes together to make bigger shapes, like joining two triangles to make a rectangle.
Your child can put together triangles, rectangles, and squares to make a new shape.
Your child can see that putting two smaller shapes like triangles, rectangles, or squares together can make a new shape.
Your child can make simple shapes by combining other basic shapes, like making a rectangle from two triangles.
Your child can match shapes that can be put together to make new shapes, like triangles making diamonds or squares.
canonical_name: Kindergarten Math: Geometry — Composing Shapes
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on composing shapes, but one mentions finding area by decomposing shapes, which is a higher-level concept.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can find the number that adds to another number from 1 to 9 to make 10, using objects or drawings, and write it down.
Your child can find a number that is one more or one less than a given number up to 20.
Your child can find the number that adds to a given number to make 5 or 10 using objects or drawings and write the answer as a drawing or equation.
Your child can use objects to find the number that adds to a number from 1 to 9 to make 10.
Your child can find different ways to make a number from 0 to 5 by adding two numbers together.
Your child can find the number that needs to be added to any number from 1 to 9 to make 10.
Your child can find different pairs of numbers that add up to a number between 0 and 10.
Your child can find the number that adds to a given number from 1 to 9 to make 10.
canonical_name: Kindergarten Math: Addition — Composing Numbers to 10
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on decomposing and composing numbers within 10, but one standard emphasizes finding one more/less up to 20, which is a related but distinct concept.
split_topics: Finding one more/one less up to 20

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can name shapes they see and describe where things are using words like above, below, beside, in front of, behind, and next to.
Your child can describe things around them using shape names and words about where things are, like above, below, or next to.
Your child can use words like above, below, beside, next to, and under to describe where 2D shapes are.
Your child can tell the position of objects in a sequence using words like first, second, third, fourth, or fifth.
Your child can use words like above, below, beside, next to, and under to describe where 3D shapes are.
Your child can name shapes they see and describe where they are using words like above, below, beside, in front of, behind, and next to.
Your child uses words like above, below, beside, in front of, behind, and next to to say where things are.
Your child uses tools like globes, maps, and GPS to describe places.
canonical_name: Kindergarten Math: Geometry — Shape Names and Positional Words
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on describing shapes and their positions, but some emphasize naming shapes, others on ordinal position, and one on using tools like maps and GPS, which is distinct.
split_topics: Naming and describing shapes with positional words
Describing position in a sequence (ordinal numbers)

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can look at flat and solid shapes of different sizes and describe how they are alike and different, including parts like sides and corners.
Your child uses tools to identify, describe, and draw special four-sided shapes and sorts flat shapes based on their features.
Your child can look at and compare flat and solid shapes of different sizes and positions, and talk about how they are alike or different, including parts like sides and corners.
Your child can find and draw lines of symmetry on flat shapes by folding them to see if the parts match.
Your child can divide circles and rectangles into two or four equal parts and use words like halves, fourths, and quarters to describe them, understanding that more parts mean smaller pieces.
Your child can divide shapes into equal parts and say the area of each part as a fraction of the whole shape.
Your child can sort flat shapes by looking for parallel or perpendicular lines and specific angle sizes, and can recognize right triangles.
Your child can sort shapes by their features and understand that shapes with shared features belong to bigger groups, and can give examples or non-examples.
canonical_name: Kindergarten Math: Geometry — Shape Attributes and Classification
coherence_reason: The standards cover related geometry concepts but include three distinct subtopics: shape attributes/classification, symmetry, and partitioning into equal parts (fractions).

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child understands that each number name you say when counting means one more than the number before.
Your child knows number names and the order of counting.
Your child understands that when you say numbers in order, each number is one more than the one before.
Your child understands number names and the order of counting numbers.
Your child understands that each next number name means one more than the last.
Your child can work with numbers that come one after another in counting.
Your child understands that each number in counting means there are more objects.
Your child knows that numbers 1 through 9 are less than ten.
canonical_name: Kindergarten Math: Counting — Number Names and One More
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on number names, counting order, and the concept of 'one more,' but one standard about 1-9 being less than ten is only tangentially related.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can break apart and put together numbers from 11 to 19 using groups of ten and extra ones, and show this with drawings or equations.
Your child can break numbers up to 10 into pairs in different ways and show this with drawings or equations.
Your child works with numbers 11 to 19 to understand how place value works.
Your child can put together and take apart numbers up to 10 using objects and pictures.
Your child can break apart numbers up to 10 in different ways using objects or drawings and show each way with pictures or simple equations.
Your child works with numbers 11 to 19 to learn about place value.
Your child can break apart numbers from 11 to 19 into ten and some more ones using objects or drawings and write this as a drawing or equation.
Your child can show numbers from 10 to 19 using one group of ten and some extra ones with objects, drawings, or words.
canonical_name: Kindergarten Math: Numbers — Composing and Decomposing Numbers to 19
coherence_reason: All standards focus on composing and decomposing numbers, but there is a minor distinction between work with numbers up to 10 and numbers 11–19 (place value).

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can look at, compare, create, and put together different shapes.
Your child learns about shapes by looking at their parts and how they fit together, and practices dividing shapes to understand fractions.
Your child uses tools to explore the features of triangles and circles.
Your child can observe and describe shapes by their important features, sort and draw them, and begin learning about fractions by dividing shapes.
Your child can draw and identify points, lines, line segments, rays, different types of angles, and lines that are perpendicular, parallel, or intersecting.
Your child can draw shapes on a coordinate plane using points and find side lengths when points share an x or y coordinate.
Your child can explore, compare, create, and put shapes together.
Your child can sort, compare, and put shapes together.
canonical_name: Kindergarten Math: Geometry — Shapes and Their Attributes
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on exploring, comparing, and composing basic shapes, but two standards introduce advanced concepts (coordinate plane, points, lines, angles) that are not developmentally appropriate for Kindergarten and represent a distinct subtopic.
split_topics: Identifying Lines, Angles, and Coordinate Plane

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can match shapes like circles, squares, rectangles, and triangles when they are the same size and facing the same way.
Your child can recognize and talk about shapes like squares, circles, triangles, rectangles, hexagons, cubes, cones, cylinders, and spheres.
Your child can name shapes correctly no matter how they are turned or how big they are.
Your child can identify and talk about shapes like squares, circles, triangles, rectangles, hexagons, cubes, cones, cylinders, and spheres.
Your child can name shapes no matter how they are turned or how big they are.
Your child can identify and draw shapes by their features, like how many angles or equal sides they have, including triangles, quadrilaterals, pentagons, hexagons, and cubes.
Your child understands the important features that define shapes, like a triangle having three sides, and can build and draw shapes showing those features.
Your child understands that if a shape has certain features, all shapes inside that group also have those features.
canonical_name: Kindergarten Math: Geometry — Identifying and Describing Shapes
coherence_reason: All standards focus on recognizing, naming, and describing basic shapes and their defining attributes, though some emphasize matching or drawing and others focus more on categorization and understanding defining features.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child understands the difference between flat shapes (2D) and solid shapes (3D).
Your child can tell if a shape is flat (2D) or solid (3D).
Your child understands the difference between flat shapes and solid shapes.
Your child can sort pictures of flat shapes and real solid objects.
Your child can explore both flat (2D) and solid (3D) shapes.
Your child can tell the difference between a shape and a letter or number.
Your child can tell the difference between a map and a globe as ways to show where people live.
Your child can tell what makes a flat shape special, like how many sides or corners it has.
canonical_name: Kindergarten Math: Geometry — Flat and Solid Shapes
coherence_reason: Most standards are about distinguishing and exploring 2D and 3D shapes, but some address distinguishing shapes from letters/numbers or comparing maps and globes, which are separate concepts.
split_topics: Distinguishing shapes from non-shapes (letters/numbers), and maps vs globes

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can show addition and subtraction using objects, fingers, pictures, sounds, acting, talking, or writing.
Your child can show what it means to add by putting things together or subtract by taking things away in everyday situations.
Your child can show addition and subtraction using things like objects, fingers, drawings, sounds, acting, or words.
Your child can explain why addition or subtraction sentences are true by using objects or drawings.
Your child can show addition and subtraction using objects, fingers, drawings, sounds, acting, talking, or writing.
Your child works with numbers by seeing or touching them.
Your child will use sounds, sights, and touch (like fingers, tally marks, or songs) to understand addition and subtraction.
Your child can show addition and subtraction using sounds, sights, and touch like fingers, counters, or songs.
canonical_name: Kindergarten Math: Addition and Subtraction — Representing and Understanding Operations
coherence_reason: All standards focus on students representing, modeling, or demonstrating addition and subtraction in various concrete and representational ways.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can solve simple addition and subtraction story problems using objects or drawings.
Your child can add and subtract numbers up to 10 by using objects or drawings to solve simple story problems.
Your child can explore adding two numbers from 0 to 10 and understand how subtraction relates to addition.
Your child can show that an addition or subtraction problem with numbers up to 5 is correct using objects or drawings.
Your child can solve simple addition and subtraction problems using objects, drawings, or number sentences.
Your child can solve simple addition and subtraction problems up to 5 using objects, drawings, or number sentences.
Your child can add and subtract one-digit numbers with sums up to 10 and use related facts to solve problems.
Your child can solve simple addition and subtraction story problems with numbers up to 10.
canonical_name: Kindergarten Math: Addition and Subtraction — Story Problems and Representations
coherence_reason: All standards focus on solving simple addition and subtraction problems within 10 using objects, drawings, or number sentences, especially in the context of story problems.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child understands how numbers and amounts are connected and that counting tells how many things there are.
Your child understands how numbers relate to amounts and connects counting to knowing how many things there are.
Your child understands what limits of functions mean both in graphs and in formal terms, including limits from one side, infinite limits, and limits as values get very large or small.
Your child understands how to use two number lines that cross to find and name points using pairs of numbers called coordinates.
Your child understands ordinal numbers like first through tenth to describe position and size.
Your child understands numbers and amounts up to 20 and connects counting to how many things there are.
Your child understands that when someone asks 'how many,' it means counting numbers.
Your child understands how numbers and amounts up to 20 are connected by counting and knowing the total amount.
canonical_name: Kindergarten Math: Numbers and Counting — Connecting Numbers to Quantities
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on connecting counting to quantities up to 20, though one references ordinal numbers which is related, and two are unrelated (limits of functions, coordinates).

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can count out loud from one to ten.
Your child can count forward starting from any number they know, not just from 1.
Your child can count to 100 by ones and by tens.
Your child can start counting forward from any number they already know.
Your child can say numbers up to 100 by ones and tens, and count forward and backward within 100 and 20.
Your child can say number names from 1 to 100 by ones and by tens from 10 to 100, and count forward to 20 or backward within 10 starting at any number.
Your child can count to 100 by ones starting from any number, not just from 1.
Your child can count to 100 by ones and also by tens.
canonical_name: Kindergarten Math: Counting — Counting Forward and Backward to 100 by Ones and Tens
coherence_reason: All standards are about counting numbers up to 100 by ones and tens, including starting from any number and sometimes counting backward within a small range.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can describe and compare things about objects that can be measured.
Your child can describe things about objects that can be measured, like how long or heavy they are, and talk about several of these features for one object.
Your child can describe things you can measure, like length or weight, pick the right tool to measure, and talk about different measurable features of one object.
Your child can describe and compare things that can be measured.
Your child can identify things about an object that can be measured, like how long, heavy, or full it is.
Your child can explore and notice things about an object that can be measured, like how long or heavy it is.
Your child can describe things like length or weight using the right words.
Your child can describe and compare sizes or amounts of things.
canonical_name: Kindergarten Math: Measurement — Describing and Comparing Measurable Attributes
coherence_reason: All standards focus on describing and comparing measurable attributes such as length or weight in objects, with no unrelated subtopics.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can compare two objects to see which one has more or less of something like length or weight and explain the difference.
Your child can sort objects by size or weight, like big or small, heavy or light.
Your child can compare old and current maps of Alabama to learn about its weather, land, waterways, transportation, politics, economy, land use, and population.
Your child can compare two objects to see which has more or less of something measurable and describe the difference.
Your child can compare two objects that share a measurable feature and describe which is longer, heavier, or bigger.
Your child can compare two objects to say which one is longer or shorter, or heavier or lighter.
Your child can tell which object is first, second, or third in a line.
Your child can compare two objects by size or amount and explain the difference.
canonical_name: Kindergarten Math: Measurement — Comparing Measurable Attributes
coherence_reason: Most standards are about comparing measurable attributes such as length or weight, but one standard is about map comparison (social studies), and another is about ordinal position, which is a distinct subtopic.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can compare two numbers from 1 to 10 written as numerals to see which is bigger or smaller.
Compare numbers.
Your child can use words like 'more' or 'less' to compare two numbers up to 20 written as numerals.
Your child can look at two numbers from 1 to 10 and say which is bigger, smaller, or if they are the same.
Your child can find, put in order, and compare numbers from 0 to 20 using a number line and words like less than, equal to, or greater than.
Your child can find and compare two numbers from 0 to 10 to say which is smaller, bigger, or if they are the same.
Your child can compare two written numbers between 1 and 10.
Your child can compare numbers and amounts to see which is bigger or smaller.
canonical_name: Kindergarten Math: Numbers — Comparing Numbers
coherence_reason: All standards focus on comparing numbers (written as numerals) within a small range using terms like 'greater than', 'less than', or 'equal to'.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can count objects so that each object gets one number, and each number is used for only one object.
Your child counts objects by saying number names in order, matching one number to each object and one object to each number.
Your child can count objects by saying number names in order, matching one number to each object and one object to each number.
Your child can count objects by matching one number to each object.
Your child knows number names and can say and write numbers in order.
Your child can count objects out loud with help, pointing to one object at a time.
Your child will practice counting things.
Your child can say each number while counting objects one by one.
canonical_name: Kindergarten Math: Counting — One-to-One Correspondence
coherence_reason: All standards focus on counting objects with one-to-one correspondence and saying number names in order.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child knows that the last number said when counting tells how many objects there are, no matter how they are arranged or counted.
Your child understands that the last number said when counting tells how many objects there are, no matter how they are arranged or counted.
Your child knows the last number said when counting tells how many objects there are, no matter how they are arranged or counted.
Your child can say the last number counted in a group of objects.
Your child knows that the last number counted is the total number of objects.
Your child can say the last number they counted when counting objects.
Your child understands that when counting objects, the last number said is the total number of objects, no matter how they are arranged.
Your child understands that the number of objects stays the same no matter how they are arranged or counted.
canonical_name: Kindergarten Math: Counting — Cardinality and Conservation
coherence_reason: All standards consistently focus on understanding that the last number said when counting represents the total quantity in a set, regardless of arrangement.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can write numbers from 0 to 20 and use these numbers to show how many objects there are, including zero for none.
Your child can write numbers from 0 to 20 and match written numbers to the right number of objects, including zero for no objects.
Your child can write numbers from 0 to 20 and use numbers to show how many objects there are, with 0 meaning none.
Your child can find the right number for a group of objects up to 20, including zero.
Your child can write or recognize numbers from 11 to 20 in any order when you say the number name.
Your child can name numbers from 0 to 10.
Your child matches a spoken number to the right number of objects up to 20.
Your child can recognize numbers up to 19.
canonical_name: Kindergarten Math: Numbers — Writing, Recognizing, and Counting 0-20
coherence_reason: All standards focus on early number sense including writing, recognizing, naming, and matching numbers to quantities up to 20, though some emphasize different ranges (0-10, 0-19, 0-20, 11-20).

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can sort objects into categories and count how many are in each category.
Your child can sort objects into groups, count how many are in each group, and put the groups in order by size.
Your child can divide a rectangle into rows and columns of equal squares and count how many squares there are.
Your child can sort objects into groups based on their features and count how many are in each group, up to 10.
Your child can sort objects into groups and count how many are in each group.
Your child can sort objects by things like size, shape, or color, count how many are in each group, and tell you the results.
Your child can collect and sort objects into groups, count how many are in each group, and tell the results by talking, writing numbers, or drawing.
Your child can group objects by categories, count how many are in each group, and arrange the groups by size.
canonical_name: Kindergarten Math: Sorting and Counting — Categorizing Objects and Comparing Quantities
coherence_reason: All but one standard focus on sorting and counting objects into groups; one standard about dividing rectangles into rows and columns is related to counting but involves a different skill.
split_topics: Dividing rectangles into rows and columns

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can tell if one group has more, less, or the same number of objects as another group when the difference is clear.
Your child can tell if one group has more, less, or the same number of objects as another group by matching or counting.
Your child can compare groups of objects, each with up to 20 items, using words like 'more,' 'less,' or 'equal.'
Your child can tell if one group of up to 10 objects has more, less, or the same number as another group.
Your child can compare two groups of objects (up to 10) to say which has more, less, or if they are equal.
Your child can compare two groups of objects with up to 20 items using words like less than, equal to, or greater than.
Your child can tell if one group has more, fewer, or the same number of objects as another group.
Your child can compare two groups of objects and say which has more, less, or if they are the same.
canonical_name: Kindergarten Math: Comparing Numbers — More, Less, and Equal Groups
coherence_reason: All standards describe comparing two groups of objects to determine which has more, less, or the same number, with only minor variations in group size limits.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can count to find out how many objects there are.
Your child can count up to 20 things arranged in a line, rectangle, or circle, or up to 10 things scattered, and can count out a given number of objects up to 20.
Your child can count objects to find out how many there are.
Your child can count up to 20 things in a line, rectangle, or circle, and count up to 10 things that are scattered; they can also count out a specific number of objects from 1 to 20.
Your child can count out a number of objects from 0 to 10.
Your child can measure how long something is up to 10 units by lining up objects without gaps or overlaps.
Your child can measure how long something is up to 20 units by lining up objects end to end without gaps or overlaps.
Your child can count a group of up to 10 objects, say how many there are, write the number, and know the number stays the same even if the objects are moved.
canonical_name: Kindergarten Math: Counting and Cardinality — Counting Objects
coherence_reason: Most standards are about counting objects, but two standards are about using objects to measure length, introducing a distinct measurement concept.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can recognize groups of 0 to 5 objects without counting.
Your child can recognize groups of 6 to 10 objects without counting.
Your child understands that when counting objects, each object matches one number name, the last number said tells how many objects there are, and can recognize small groups up to 5 without counting.
Your child can recognize and combine small groups of objects that add up to 5 without counting each one.
Your child can recognize groups of 1 to 10 objects arranged in patterns and say how many there are without counting.
Your child can recognize how many objects are in a group up to 5 without counting, especially when they are arranged in common patterns.
Your child can quickly tell how many objects there are in small groups up to 10 without counting one by one, by recognizing patterns or parts.
Your child can quickly recognize how many objects there are in a small group (up to 5) without counting.
canonical_name: Kindergarten Math: Numbers — Subitizing Small Groups
coherence_reason: All standards focus on the ability to recognize the number of objects in small groups (up to 5 or 10) without counting, which is the concept of subitizing.
